coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe constructed using metal–organic frameworks and nitrogen-doped carbon dots for specific detection of adenosine monophosphate
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tian
Tan, Qin
Chen, Runchun
Gan, Zhiwen
Zhen, Shujun
Hu, Xiaoli
Peng, Huanjun
coredata.dc:description: The selective detection of adenosine monophosphate (AMP) is of great significance for the study of cellular energy metabolism. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed based on europium-based metal–organic frameworks (Eu-MOF) and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) that could specifically recognize AMP. Europium nitrate and Disodium Terephthalate (Na2BDC) were used to synthesize Eu-BDC with red fluorescence. The fluorescence of the Eu-BDC was effectively suppressed due to water molecules coordinating with the Eu3+. However, the addition of AMP resulted in a significant restoration of the fluorescence of Eu-BDC, while the fluorescence remained suppressed upon addition of adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). We developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor that could accurately detect AMP by utilizing the blue fluorescence emitted from CDs as a reliable reference signal. Under optimal conditions, the Eu-BDC/CDs system for AMP detection exhibited a linear detection range of 1.0–80.0 μmol/L and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.25 μmol/L. The method displayed simple preparation, high selectivity and low cost for AMP detection. To our knowledge, this is the first highly-specify double-response biosensor for AMP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Eu-MOF
Specific
AMP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence method detection of AMP
Possible mechanism for ratiometric determination of AMP
Selectivity of the fluorescence probe for AMP detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of Eu-BDC. (B) XRD image of Eu-BDC. (C) The full scan XPS spectrum of Eu-BDC. (D) The XPS spectrum of Eu 3d. (E) The FTIR of Eu-BDC.
The effect of different condition towards system (A) reaction time (2–50 min). (B) the pH of buffer solutions. (C) The volume of Eu-BDC solution (0.5 mg/mL, V CDs = 100 µL, C AMP = 60 μmol/L).

Detection of AMP (A) The ratiometric fluorescence spectra of AMP at different concentrations between 1.0 and 80.0 μmol/L. (B) Linear relationship between F 620/F 445 and the concentration of AMP (1.0–80.0 μmol/L). (C) Color changes of systems with different AMP concentrations.

(A) Fluorescence excitation, emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (B) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of Eu-BDC. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-BDC/CDs dissolved in water (0, 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15 min). (D) The fuorescence spectrum of Eu-BDC/CDs, Eu-BDC/CDs + AD, Eu-BDC/CDs + AMP, Eu-BDC/CDs + ATP and Eu-BDC/CDs + ADP (V CDs = 100.0 µL, V Eu-BDC = 90.0 µL, the concentration of AMP, ADP, ATP and AD were 40.0 μmol/L).
The sensing mechanism diagram of detecting AMP based on Eu-BDC/CDs system.
Comparison of different methods for AMP detection.
Determination of AMP in the injection samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe constructed using metal–organic frameworks and nitrogen-doped carbon dots for specific detection of adenosine monophosphate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SAic-GM-Wuling Automobile Corporation, Liuzhou 545007, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: SAic-GM-Wuling Automobile Corporation, Liuzhou 545007, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selective detection of adenosine monophosphate (AMP) is of great significance for the study of cellular energy metabolism. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed based on europium-based metal–organic frameworks (Eu-MOF) and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) that could specifically recognize AMP. Europium nitrate and Disodium Terephthalate (Na2BDC) were used to synthesize Eu-BDC with red fluorescence. The fluorescence of the Eu-BDC was effectively suppressed due to water molecules coordinating with the Eu3+. However, the addition of AMP resulted in a significant restoration of the fluorescence of Eu-BDC, while the fluorescence remained suppressed upon addition of adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). We developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor that could accurately detect AMP by utilizing the blue fluorescence emitted from CDs as a reliable reference signal. Under optimal conditions, the Eu-BDC/CDs system for AMP detection exhibited a linear detection range of 1.0–80.0 μmol/L and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.25 μmol/L. The method displayed simple preparation, high selectivity and low cost for AMP detection. To our knowledge, this is the first highly-specify double-response biosensor for AMP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Adenine nucleotides mainly contain small molecules such as adenosine triphosphate (ATP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and adenosine monophosphate (AMP), which play an important signaling role in cellular energy metabolism [1,2]. ADP could be generated through hydrolysis of ATP. ATP could also be hydrolyzed into AMP and pyrophosphate (ppi) through pyrophosphorylation [3,4]. AMP exhibits remarkable multifunctionality as it serves not only as a regulator of carbohydrate metabolism both externally and internally to cells and of signal transduction processes, but also as an indispensable constituent of RNA synthesis [5,6]. Abnormal concentrations of AMP inside the cell are often associated with some diseases. Therefore, selective detection of AMP is of great significance for studying energy metabolism mechanisms. However, the specific detection of AMP can be challenging due to the simultaneous presence of ATP and its hydrolysis products, which could interfere with accurate measurement. Several methods are available for detecting AMP, including high-performance liquid chromatography [7], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [8], colorimetry [9–10], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [11] and electrochemical [12]. However, some of these methods require time-consuming sample pretreatment or complex synthesis processes, which could influence the efficiency of the detection process. Fluorescent probes, due to their rapid response and easy handling, are widely utilized in medical diagnosis and biological analysis [13]. Hence, it is imperative to design a fluorescent probe that is dependable, swift, and uncomplicated to differentiate AMP from other forms of adenosine.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), also called porous coordination polymers, are a type of porous crystalline materials formed by coordinating metal nodes and organic ligands [14]. They have advantages like diverse structures, large specific surface area and adjustable pore sizes, making them extensively used in biosensors [15]. Among them, lanthanide-based metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) are a new type of luminescent material composed of Ln3+ or lanthanide metal clusters and organic ligands. They have excellent luminescent properties, including long fluorescence lifetime, large Stokes shift, narrow characteristic emission peak, and so on [16,17]. Due to these advantages, Ln-MOFs show great potential in the application of fluorescence sensors. Single-signal fluorescence probes based on Ln-MOFs are susceptible to interference from external environmental factors, and this might lead to inaccurate detection results. To solve this problem, ratiometric fluorescence sensors with self-calibration capability have been designed and applied, which can improve the signal-to-noise ratio and sensitivity of the detection method [18]. Carbon dots (CDs) possess numerous advantages, comprising of low toxicity, remarkable stability and eco-friendliness [19–21]. Therefore, CDs is often used as a reference signal to construct ratiometric fluorescence probes. An et al developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe to detect xanthine employing boron-doped carbon dots (B-CQDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [22]. Based on the above work, CDs and Eu-MOFs, as fluorescent materials with excellent luminescent properties, play an important role in constructing high-performance, highly sensitive and selectively ratiometric fluorescence sensors. In this study, our aim is to integrate carbon dots and Eu-MOFs to construct a dual-emission probe for the specific detection of AMP.

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence probe is designed by physically mixing carbon dots and Eu-MOF together, and use it for specific detection of AMP. The Eu-MOF (Eu-BDC) is synthesized containing disodium terephthalate (Na2BDC) and europium nitrate. The sensing scheme is shown in Scheme 1 , where the coordination between Eu-BDC and water molecules leads to fluorescence quenching. The phosphate of AMP has a stronger coordination ability with Eu3+, which breaks the coordination between Eu-BDC and water molecules, leading to the fluorescence recovery of Eu-BDC. At the same time, carbon dots with stable fluorescence properties can serve as a reference signal and provide color contrast. The established method show that the fluorescence probe exhibit high specificity. Based on the above, this work provides an innovative ratiometric fluorescence-based detection method for AMP.

Uranium nitrate, Na2BDC, AMP, ATP, ADP, alanine (Ala), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), glucose (GO), leucine (Leu), Histidine (His), Glutamate (Glu), Arginine (Arg), threonine (Thr) were produced by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Corporation. Citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Chongqing North Beibei Chemical Reagent Corporation)(Chongqing, China). Adenosine (AD) was purchased from SANLAND (Xiamen, China). All the reagents were analytical grade and were directly used without further treatments. The deionized water was used in the whole experiment.

The infrared spectra were recorded using a Nicolet IS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (FEI, USA) for the detailed characterization of the size, shape, and morphology of the CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained using an AXS-D8 X-ray diffractometer manufactured by Bruker in Germany. The elemental distribution and valence states on the surface of Eu-BDC were obtained using Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from Thermo Scientific (USA). The UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively.

The modified procedure, as outlined in the literature [23], involved dissolving 0.4 g of citric acid and 2.0 mL of ethylenediamine in 10 mL of deionized water. Ultrasonic treatment was then performed to ensure complete dissolution. Then transferred to a 20.0 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reaction flask. The reaction should be carried out at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, purify the solution using a 1000 Da dialysis bag and change the water every 4 h for 24 h. Store the purified solution at 4 °C.

Synthesis of Eu-BDC following previously published literature [24]. 0.25 mmol/L of Na2BDC and 0.25 mmol/L of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in 3.0 mL of deionized water, then was performed at 1000 rpm/min for 30 min with magnetic stirring. The stirring was stopped and the white precipitate was obtained by centrifugation. The white precipitate was washed three times with deionized water and acetone. Finally, the obtained Eu-BDC was dried at 60 °C. The dried Eu-BDC was then prepared as 0.5 mg/mL solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence method detection of AMP
ce:section.ce:para: After mixing 100.0 μL of CDs solution, 100.0 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.5, 0.05 mol/L) and 90.0 μL of Eu-MOF solution (0.5 mg/mL), different concentrations of AMP solution were added and the final mixture was made up to 1.0 mL with deionized water. After 30 min, the emission wavelength at 445 nm and 620 nm were recorded under excitation at 280 nm.

The TEM image displayed in Fig. S1 A showed that the CDs were uniformly dispersed and had a spherical shape. The average particle size of the CDs was determined to be 1.84 nm, with a range of distribution between 0.2 and 3.4 nm, as illustrated in Fig. S1B. The lattice spacing of CDs was 0.219 nm as seen in Fig. S1C. The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. S1D) indicated the presence of the carbon-core structure with a diffraction peak at 22.7° [25,26]. The chemical bonds on the surface of CDs were analyzed by infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Absorption peaks at 2959.5 cm−1 and 1273.9 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H bond and C-O [27,28]. Absorption peaks around 1571.8 cm−1 and 1384.4 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibrations of N–H/C = C bonds as shown in Fig. S1E [29].

The morphology and microstructure of the Eu-BDC were presented in Fig. 1A. The image showed that the Eu-MOF was comprised of ellipsoidal particles, which were uniformly dispersed. It had a small central nanoparticle with a length of 0.4 µm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the diffraction peaks of Eu-BDC were consistent with previous research [30]. The XPS full spectra of Eu-BDC, as shown in Fig. 1C and D, confirmed the existence of Eu, O and C elements. The characteristic peaks of Eu 3d, C 1 s and O 1 s were located at 1134.97, 284.88, and 532.26 eV, respectively. The two peaks at 1135.0 eV (Eu 3d5/2) and 1165.0 eV (Eu 3d3/2) were the doublet peaks of Eu 3d, which confirmed the presence Eu3+. Fig. 1E exhibited the IR spectrum of Eu-BDC which revealed a strong absorption peak at 3454.5 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H of water molecules, indicating that water not only serves as a solvent but also as a reactant [31]. The distinctive peaks at 1542.7 cm−1 and 1420.0 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of the carboxylic group [32]. Overall, Eu-BDC was successfully synthesized.

The effects of reaction time, pH value, and volume of Eu-BDC solution on the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of the Eu-BDC/CDs system were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of the system, which was the difference in F 620/F 445 with and without AMP, remained constant as the reaction time exceeds 30 min. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity difference of the Eu-BDC/CDs system increased as the pH increased from 7.1 to 7.5. However, once the pH exceeded 7.5, the fluorescence intensity began to decline. Fig. 2C illustrated that the Eu-BDC/CDs system achieved its highest fluorescence intensity when the volume of Eu-BDC solution was at 90.0 µL. In summary, the ideal reaction conditions were 30 min reaction time, pH 7.5 and Eu-BDC solution volume 90.0 µL. Subsequently, all other experiments were performed under optimal conditions.

To accurately determine AMP, the concentration of AMP was quantitatively added to the Eu-BDC/CDs system. As shown in Fig. 3 A, with AMP concentration increasing gradually, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained basically unchanged, while the fluorescence intensity of Eu-BDC gradually increased. Fig. 3B indicated that there was a linear correlation between the ratio of F 620/F 445 and the concentration within the range of 1.0–80.0 μmol/L, with the linear equation being F 620/F 445 = 0.09081C AMP + 0.3141 (R2 = 0.9979). The limit of detection (LOD) for the system was measured to be 0.25 μmol/L based on 3σ/k rule. Fig. 3C demonstrated the color changes of the ratiometric fluorescence system when subjected to diverse concentrations of AMP. The fluorescence color of the system changed from blue to red under UV light, implying a gradual shift in the color as the concentration of AMP increased. This change in color was indicative that AMP could be measured based on the color transitions. Moreover, in comparison to previously reported probes in the Table 1 , this method demonstrated advantages such as simple preparation, high selectivity and low costs, making it promising for the selective detection of AMP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for ratiometric determination of AMP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 A, the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs was 355 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was 445 nm. As depicted in Fig. 4B, The maximum excitation wavelength of Eu-BDC was 280 nm, and it exhibited a strong fluorescence emission peak at 620 nm. This was due to the transition of Eu3+ from 5D0 to 7F2 states, which was sensitized by the ligand and results in the strong luminescence of Eu3+. In order to make the fluorescence signal of Eu-BDC more perceptible, 280 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for the entire system.

Fig. 4C displayed the variation in fluorescence intensity over time upon dissolving Eu-BDC/CDs in water. As illustrated in the Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-BDC/CDs system at 620 nm quickly diminished from 0 min and eventually stabilized after 15 min. While the fluorescence signal of CDs at 445 nm stayed nearly constant, the fluorescence signal of Eu-BDC decreased significantly, indicative of water molecule-induced quenching of Eu-BDC fluorescence. Previous studies suggested that this influence could be attributed to the coupling of Eu3+ excited states to the O–H of water molecules, with the energy of the Eu3+ excited state transferring to the O–H of water molecules and, consequently, quenching the red fluorescence of Eu3+ [37,38].

As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was almost no obvious response to AMP and could be used as a reference signal. After adding AMP, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-BDC was significantly enhanced. Upon adding ATP and ADP to the Eu-BDC/CDs system, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-BDC was slightly quenched. This may be due to the presence of three and two phosphate groups in ATP and ADP, respectively, which can destroy the structure of MOFs and hinder the energy transfer process between the BDC and metal ions [5,40]. Based on the different fluorescence responses of AMP, ATP and ADP in the system, the Eu-BDC/CDs ratiometric fluorescent system could be used for selective detection of AMP.

The next study mainly investigated the reason for the increase in Eu-BDC fluorescence caused by AMP. In general, the energy transfer between the target and the Eu3+formed an antenna effect that lead to fluorescence enhancement. However, the triplet energy level of AMP was lower than the resonant level 5D0 of Eu3+, thereby preventing energy transfer from AMP to Eu3+ [39]. Therefore, the mechanism of AMP-induced Eu-BDC fluorescence enhancement was not energy transfer. Subsequently, the influence of AD on the fluorescence intensity of Eu-BDC was explored. AD could not restore the fluorescence of Eu-BDC, indicating that the single phosphate group in AMP played an important role (Fig. 4D). It had been reported that Eu3+ possessed a high affinity for oxygen donors such as phosphates [5,40]. The affinity between Eu3+ and P-O was stronger than that between Eu3+ and O–H of water molecules. After AMP was added to the system, the fluorescence was restored because AMP replaced the water molecule and coordinated with Eu3+, resulting in fluorescence enhancement [5].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the fluorescence probe for AMP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure effective measurement of the target analyte, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor should possess higher selectivity for the specific analyte in comparison to other coexisting species. The examination was conducted under optimal reaction conditions. The effects of substances such as K+, Ca2+, Na+, PO4 3-, Cys, GSH, Ala, Leu, His, Glu, GO, Arg, Thr, ATP and ADP on the determination system of Eu-BDC/CDs are shown in Fig. S2. When the concentration of AMP was 40.0 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of the system was mostly unaffected, except when the concentrations of ATP, PO4 3- and ADP were 10 times that of AMP, and the concentrations of other coexisting species are 100 times that of AMP. Therefore, this method showed good selectivity for the determination of AMP.

The precision of the proposed methods was investigated by calculating intra-day and inter-day precision. The responses of Eu-BDC/CDs to different concentrations of AMP were detected at different times on the same day and three different days [41]. The RSD of intra-day precision were from 2.1% to 3.5%. The RSD of inter-day precision were from 1.8% to 3.9% (Table S1). The results showed that the methods had good precision and repeatability. Furthermore, the robustness of the method was verified by slightly changing the optimal value of pH and reaction time. The results were showed in Table S2. Although changes in pH of valve could affect the results of the method, the addition of buffer solutions to the ratiometric fluorescent sensor at optimal pH can reduce the impact. The small changes of reaction time could not influence the sensor in Table S3. The results showed that the sensor had good robustness.

To assess whether the designed method was feasible in practical application, we evaluated its capability to detect AMP in vidarabine monophosphate injection samples with the specified amount as 0.2 g/bottle (the medicine was produced by Guangdong Xianqiang Pharmaceutical Co., LTD) [42,43]. Three bottle of medicine were dissolved in 30.0 mL of water, then diluted by a certain multiple, and the final solution was used as the real sample solution. The developed method was used to determine the real sample solution, and the spike-and-recovery method experiment was carried out to further verify the accuracy of the method. Standard solutions containing 10.0, 40.0 and 70.0 μmol/LAMP were added to the test solution. The measurement results were shown in the Table 2 . The result acquired by the developed was in accord with labeled amount. Regarding the determination of AMP, the recovery was ranged from 99.60% to 103.2%, while the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 1.9% to 3.5%. The results indicated that the method possessed good accuracy and precision.

In this work, a novel ratiometric fluorescence method that utilizes the combination of two distinct fluorescence materials to produce a dual emission signal is developed. The fluorescence emission intensity of system is significantly enhanced by AMP, thus allowing for the determination of AMP. With different responses, the ratiometric fluorescence platform can selectively distinguish AMP, ATP and ADP. The method had good selectivity, high sensitivity and simple preparation. Additionally, the proposed method is helpful for monitoring the energy metabolism process inside cells, thereby enabling real-time analysis of enzyme activity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974109), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. XDJK2019TY003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109171.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
